title: SEGMENT 15 — Oncolytic adenovirus: ONYX-015, T-VEC's cousins, and the engineering tricks

lines.text: SEGMENT 15 — Oncolytic adenovirus: ONYX-015, T-VEC's cousins, and the engineering tricks
We have spent a lot of time on adenovirus as a delivery vehicle for therapeutic genes and as a vaccine platform.
There is a third major use case I want to spend a segment on, because it represents a fundamentally different conceptual approach to using viruses in medicine.
Oncolytic adenoviruses are vectors engineered to replicate selectively in cancer cells and kill them.
Not to deliver a cargo.
To be the cargo, in effect — to use the viral life cycle itself, in cancer cells specifically, as the therapy.
Let me build up the logic.
Adenovirus normally replicates in any cell it can infect, lyses the cell, and spreads.

If we could constrain that replication so that it happens only in cancer cells, we'd have a self-amplifying, tumor-targeted therapy: inject some virus into a tumor, the virus replicates in the cancer cells, kills them, releases more virus, infects neighboring cancer cells, repeats.
A wave of viral replication spreads through the tumor, leaving healthy tissue alone.
The truck doesn't just deliver a package — the truck is the package, and it makes copies of itself.
The trick is constraining the replication to cancer cells specifically.
There are several approaches.
Approach one: exploit deficiencies in tumor cell defenses.
The classical example is ONYX-015, an oncolytic adenovirus developed in the 1990s that has a deletion in the E1B-55K gene.
The wild adenoviral E1B-55K protein normally inactivates the tumor suppressor p53, allowing the virus to replicate in normal cells that would otherwise mount a p53-driven antiviral response.

The reasoning behind ONYX-015 was: if we delete E1B-55K, the virus can no longer inactivate p53, so the virus can only replicate in cells where p53 is already inactivated — that is, in cancer cells that have lost p53 function.
And p53 loss is one of the most common cancer mutations, occurring in perhaps half of all human cancers.
So ONYX-015 should replicate in p53-deficient tumor cells and be blocked in normal cells.
The reality turned out to be more complicated.

ONYX-015 did show selective replication in some tumor cells, but the selectivity wasn't strictly p53-dependent — other cellular factors influenced replication too — and clinical trials in the 1990s and 2000s showed modest activity.

ONYX-015 itself was not approved in Western markets, but a closely related construct called H101 was approved in China in 2005 for treatment of head and neck cancers, making it one of the first approved oncolytic virus therapies anywhere in the world.
Approach two: use tumor-specific promoters to drive viral genes.

You can put the essential viral E1A gene under control of a promoter that is active only in cancer cells — for example, the telomerase promoter, which is active in most cancer cells (which need telomerase to keep dividing) and largely silent in differentiated normal cells.
In normal cells, the modified vector cannot express E1A, cannot turn itself on, cannot replicate.
In cancer cells, the telomerase promoter drives E1A expression, the vector replicates, the cell lyses.
This is tumor-selective replication driven by cancer-specific transcriptional activity.
Approach three: combine oncolytic replication with immune stimulation.

The most successful oncolytic virus to reach the clinic in the United States is actually not an adenovirus — it's talimogene laherparepvec, or T-VEC, an oncolytic herpesvirus modified to express the immune-stimulating cytokine GM-CSF.
T-VEC is approved by the FDA for advanced melanoma.

The principle, though, applies to adenoviruses too: arm the oncolytic virus with a transgene encoding an immune-stimulating molecule, so that the lysed tumor cells release not just viral particles but also a cytokine that recruits immune cells to the tumor.
The lysis releases tumor antigens; the cytokine recruits cells that can present and respond to those antigens; a systemic anti-tumor immune response can develop.
The oncolytic virus, in effect, becomes an in situ vaccine — turning a tumor into its own antigen factory.
The analogy I like for oncolytics.
An oncolytic adenovirus is the truck engineered to become a weapon, but only in enemy territory.
In a normal cell — friendly territory — the engineered truck can't start its engine.
In a cancer cell — enemy territory — the engineered truck starts up, drives around making copies of itself, demolishes the building it's in, and the copies drive off to demolish the next building.
The selectivity is built into the truck's wiring, so that it only goes active where you want it to.
Oncolytic adenoviruses have had a long and somewhat frustrating clinical history.
The basic concept is appealing; the practical implementation has been hard.
Tumors are heterogeneous — different cells in the same tumor have different mutations, different receptor profiles, different replicative states.
Getting an oncolytic virus to spread efficiently through a solid tumor and reach all the relevant cells has proven difficult.
Intratumoral injection limits delivery to accessible lesions; systemic delivery brings back all the immune barriers we discussed earlier.

The field remains active — there are dozens of oncolytic adenoviruses in clinical trials worldwide — but the dramatic approval-and-deployment story we saw with COVID vaccines has not yet happened in oncolytics.
It may.
We'll see.
